[Question] In an episode reminiscent of Sheldon Cooper's myriad idiosyncrasies aired on March 26, 2010, which particular anxiety gripped him upon the realization that his triumphant procurement of the Chancellor's Award for Science necessitated a speech at a stately dinner?
[Answer]
raw_source_record.dialog_bm25_dialog:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Context:
Coversation [March 22, 2010, Session #431]:
Speaker Howardsays : Didn’t it look like that spear was going to go right through your skull?
Speaker Rajsays : No.
Speaker Leonardsays : Hey, you didn’t want a Slurpee at 7-Eleven, you don’t get glasses.
Speaker rings)says : Oh, that will be another congratulatory call for me. Uh, mute, please.
Speaker Howardsays : Uh, hang on, flaming arrow.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Hello? Oh, Chancellor Morton, how are you, sir? Yes, I was expecting your call  three years ago. I see. Wait. What happens if I choose not to give a speech? Uh-huh. And if I don’t want to forfeit the award? Well, you’ve got that tied up in a neat little bow. All right. Thank you.  Problem.
Speaker Leonardsays : What?
Speaker Sheldonsays : They expect me to give a speech at the banquet. I can’t give a speech.
Speaker Howardsays : Well, no, you’re mistaken. You give speeches all the time. What you can’t do is shut up.
Speaker Rajsays : Yeah, before the movie, you did 20 minutes on why guacamole turns brown. It turned brown while you were talking.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I am perfectly comfortable speaking to small groups. I cannot speak to large crowds.
Speaker Leonardsays : What, to you, is a large crowd?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Any group big enough to trample me to death. General rule of thumb is 36 adults or 70 children.
Speaker Pennysays : Sheldon, congratulations. Brought you cheesecake from work. You know, ’cause of your award, not because a busboy sneezed on it.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m not accepting the award.
Speaker Pennysays : Why not?
Speaker Howardsays : Turns out the great Sheldon Cooper has stage fright.
Speaker Pennysays : That’s no reason to back out. You know, I once got a pretty big honour in high school, and I was terrified about appearing in front of a big crowd, but I went through with it, and you know what? The world looked pretty darn good sitting on a haystack in the back of a Ford F-150 as a member of the Corn Queen’s court.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Thank you. Yeah, I’ll bear that in mind if I’m ever nominated for the Hillbilly Peace Prize.
Speaker Leonardsays : Sheldon, you’re being ridiculous.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Am I? Let me tell you a story.
Speaker Howardsays : Where’s 70 children when you need ‘em?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I was 14 and graduating summa cum laude from college. Summa cum laude is Latin for with highest honours.
Speaker Pennysays : I just love how you always skip over the part where no one asks.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I was valedictorian and expected to give an address. Even now, I can remember that moment when I walked up to the podium and looked out at the crowd. There must have been thousands of people. My heart started pounding in my chest. I began to hyperventilate. My vision became blurry, and before I knew it… oh, dear.
Speaker Pennysays : Oh, my God.
Speaker Leonardsays : Sheldon? Sheldon, are you okay?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Don’t trample me.

Coversation [March 22, 2010, Session #430]:
Speaker Rajsays : Okay in Avatar when they have sex in Pandora they hook up their ponytails, so we know their ponytails are like their junk.
Speaker Howardsays : Yeah, so?
Speaker Rajsays : So, when they ride horses and fly on the birds, they also use their ponytails.
Speaker Howardsays : What’s your point?
Speaker Rajsays : My point is, if I were a horse or a bird, I’d be very nervous around James Cameron.
Speaker Sheldonsays : It amazes me how you constantly obsess over fictional details when there are more important things in the real world to worry about. For example, why wasn’t William Shatner in the new Star Trek movie?
Speaker Leonardsays : Hey, Sheldon, I was up in the administration office, and I happened to overhear the name of the winner of this year’s Chancellor’s Award for Science.
Speaker Sheldonsays : And you want to rub my nose in the fact that my contributions are being overlooked again? I am the William Shatner of theoretical physics. All right, I’ll play. What self-important, preening fraud are they honouring this year?
Speaker Leonardsays : Oh, I’m so glad you asked it like that. You.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I won?
Speaker Leonardsays : You won.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I won! This is astonishing. Not that I won the award, no one deserves it more. Actually, I guess I misspoke. It’s not astonishing, more like inevitable. I’m not sure what to do first. Maybe I should call my mother. Wait! I know, I’m going to conduct an interview with myself and post it online.
Speaker Rajsays : Well, good for him.
Speaker Howardsays : Yeah, the one thing the William Shatner of theoretical physics needed was an ego boost.

Coversation [March 25, 2010, Session #436]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Good evening, ladies and gentlemen. My name is Dr. Leonard Hofstadter, and it is my very great honour to introduce the winner of this year’s Chancellor’s award for Science and my good friend, Dr. Sheldon Cooper. But before I do, I’d like to share with you a letter from Sheldon’s mother, who couldn’t be here tonight. Isn’t that nice? His mother sent him a letter. She’s proud of him. I wonder what that feels like. Dear Shelly. That’s what she calls him. Shelly, it’s a pet name. You know what my mother’s pet name for me is? Leonard. But I digress. Dear Shelly. I am so proud of…
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, dear.
Speaker Pennysays : What’s the matter?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m getting dizzy.
Speaker Rajsays : Don’t worry. You’re surrounded by your C-Men.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I can’t do this. I’m going to faint.
Speaker Pennysays : Here, drink this. It’ll relax you.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Alcohol? I don’t drink alcohol.
Speaker Pennysays : Fine, faint.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I don’t feel different, this alcohol’s defective.
Speaker Pennysays : Here, see if this one works.
Speaker talking)says : First of all, the projects were totally different. I was showing that classical music nurtures lima beans and makes them grow, but my mother didn’t hear me. If you’d like to look at the relationship between nurturing and growth, I’d like to point out that my brother is eight inches taller than me.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m ready.
Speaker Leonardsays : Oh, right. Ladies and gentlemen, our guest of honour, Dr. Sheldon Cooper.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Thanks, shorty,I’ll take it from here. All right, you people ready to have some fun? You have a basic understanding of differential calculus and at least one year of algebraic topology? Well, then here come the jokes. Why did the chicken cross the Mobius strip? To get to the same side, bazinga! All right, a neutron walks into a bar and asks, how much for a drink? The bartender says, for you, no charge. Hello? I know you’re out there. I can hear you metabolizing oxygen and expelling carbon dioxide. Looks like we have some academic dignitaries in the audience. Dr. Randall from the geology department, only man who’s happy when they take his work for granite. Ba-da cha! I kid the geologists, of course, but it’s only ’cause I have no respect for the field. Let’s get serious for a moment. Why are we all here? ‘Cause we’re scientists. And what do scientists study? The universe. And what’s the universe made of? I am so glad you asked.  There’s antimony, arsenic, aluminium, selenium, and hydrogen and oxygen and nitrogen and rhenium, and nickel, neodymium,

Coversation [November 17, 2011, Session #709]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Hey, can I talk to you about something? It’s a little awkward.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I know what this is about. Given the professional standstill you’re at, you’re wondering if this is the appropriate time to abandon your research and focus on teaching. Yes. And if I may suggest, consider changing disciplines. Yeah, to the humanities, perhaps history. One of the advantages of teaching history is that you don’t have to create things, you know, you just have to remember stuff that happened and then parrot it back. You could have fun with that.
Speaker Leonardsays : Yeah, that’s not it. Stuart’s kind of interested in Amy.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, of course he is. She’s very interesting. Did you know, when she was 14, she severed the webbing between her own toes?
Speaker Leonardsays : No. He wanted me to find out if you’d have a problem with him asking her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m not sure how to respond, Leonard. I don’t own Amy. Can’t own a person. At least not since? 1863. President Lincoln freed the? Slaves! Come on Leonard, if you’re going to teach history, these are the kind of facts you’ll have to know.
Speaker Leonardsays : You know what? Never mind. I’m going to tell him it’s okay to ask her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : The question is moot. There is no way that Stuart, an impoverished peddler of picture books, would be at all appealing to Amy Farrah Fowler, a noted neurobiologist capable of performing surgery on her own feet with nothing but nitrous oxide from cans of whipped cream as anesthesia.
Speaker Leonardsays : All right, but just for the record, I checked in with you to see how you’d feel about it.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Fine. The record shall so reflect. Now, getting back to the problem at hand, what to do with a washed-up experimental physicist.
Speaker Leonardsays : I am not washed-up.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, Leonard. Lots of people love you and want to help you, but they can’t until you admit the problem.

Coversation [March 19, 2008, Session #64]:
Speaker Leonardsays : So, in conclusion, the data show that at temperatures approaching absolute zero, the moment of inertia changes, and the solid becomes a super-solid, which clearly appears to be a previously unknown state of matter.  Thank you.  Are there any questions?
Speaker Voicesays : Yeah. What the hell was that?
Speaker Leonardsays : Any other questions?
Speaker glasses)says : Doctor Sheldon Cooper here, I am the lead author of this particular paper.  Thank you. And you, sir, you have completely skipped over the part where I was walking through the park, and I saw these children on a merry-go-round, which started me thinking about the moment of inertia in gasses like helium at temperatures approaching absolute zero.
Speaker Leonardsays : I didn’t skip it, it’s just an anecdote. It’s not science.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, I see, was the apple falling on Newton’s head, was that just an anecdote?
Speaker Leonardsays : You are not Isaac Newton.
Speaker Sheldonsays : No, no that’s true, gravity would have been apparent to me without the apple.
Speaker Leonardsays : You cannot possibly be that arrogant.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You continue to underestimate me, my good man.
Speaker Leonardsays : Look, if you weren’t happy with my presentation then maybe you should have given it with me.
Speaker Sheldonsays : As I have explained repeatedly, unlike you, I don’t need validation from lesser minds. No offence.
Speaker Leonardsays : Really, so why did you come?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Because I knew you’d screw this up.
Speaker Leonardsays : I didn’t screw it up.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, please. I admit, that spherical chicken joke, that was hilarious. But it was straight downhill from there.
Speaker Leonardsays : I’ve had enough of your condescension. Maybe I didn’t go to college when I was eleven like you, maybe I got my doctorate at 24 instead of 16, but you are not the only person who is smarter than everyone else in this room. No offense. And I am clearly not the only person who is tormented by insecurity and has an ego in need of constant validation.
Speaker Sheldonsays : So you admit that you’re an egotist?
Speaker Leonardsays : Yes.  My name is Dr Leonard Hofstadter, and I could never please my parents so I need to get all my self-esteem from strangers like you. But he’s worse.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Okay, that is it.
Speaker Leonardsays : You cannot blow up my head with your mind.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Then I’ll settle for an aneurysm.
Speaker down)says : Stop it.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You hit me. You saw him, he hit me.
Speaker Leonardsays : You were trying to blow up my head.
Speaker Sheldonsays : So it was working.
Speaker Leonardsays : It wasn’t, it was not, you are a nutcase.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh we’ll see about that , heads up you people in the front row, this is a splash zone.
Speaker Leonardsays : Stop, stop it, quit it.
Speaker Pennysays : Is this usually how these physics things go?
Speaker Howardsays : More often than you’d think.
Speaker floor)says : Vulcan nerve pinch!

Speaker Sheldonsays : I was valedictorian and expected to give an address. Even now, I can remember that moment when I walked up to the podium and looked out at the crowd. Speaker Leonardsays : Sheldon? Sheldon, are you okay?

Coversation [October 10, 2010, Session #489]:

Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, gentlemen.

Coversation [March 23, 2010, Session #432]:

Speaker Pennysays : Yes, but you’re not smarter than all of us put together.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, I’m sorry, that is what I meant.
Speaker Pennysays : Okay, your problem is, you’re trying to do this all by yourself.
Speaker Leonardsays : We can help you. We can be your team. Like, uh, Professor Xavier and his X-Men.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I do like the X-Men.

Speaker Leonardsays : Yeah, we watched it last week. You said you liked it.
Speaker Pennysays : Oh. I say a lot of things, sweetie. So, how about it, Sheldon?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I don’t know. If you’re my X-Men, what are your powers?
Speaker Pennysays : Okay. Well, I am going to take you shopping, get you a nice suit. Might give you more confidence.
Speaker Sheldonsays : That’s not exactly a mutation that would get you into Xavier’s School for Gifted Youngsters, but go on. Leonard?
Speaker Leonardsays : I thought I could try to analyze you and get to the root of your anxiety.
Speaker Sheldonsays : What qualifies you to attempt to understand my mind?
Speaker Leonardsays : My mother is a highly regarded psychiatrist, and I’ve been in therapy ever since she accused me of breast-feeding co-dependently.
Speaker Howardsays : Raj says he can teach you, what did you call it? I don’t know, some Indian meditation crap.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I see. Well, I assume, since the rest of you have set the bar so low, you’re saving the most impressive contribution for last. Go ahead, Howard. Dazzle me.
Speaker Howardsays : My power is the ability to pretend like I give a damn about your piddly-ass problems. And that’s 24-7, buddy.
Speaker Sheldonsays : And I appreciate the pretence.
Speaker Pennysays : So, what do you say, Sheldon? Are we your X-Men?
Speaker Sheldonsays : No. The X-Men were named for the X in Charles Xavier. Since I am Sheldon Cooper, you will be my C-Men.
Speaker Howardsays : Oh, that’s not a good name.

Speaker box)says : Oh, dear.  Oh, dear.  Oh, dear, oh, dear. Oh, dear, oh, dear. Oh, dear. Oh, dear. Purell, Purell, Purell, Purell.
Speaker (entering)says : Good morning, Sheldon.

Coversation [March 22, 2010, Session #430]:
Speaker Rajsays : Okay in Avatar when they have sex in Pandora they hook up their ponytails, so we know their ponytails are like their junk.
Speaker Howardsays : Yeah, so?
Speaker Rajsays : So, when they ride horses and fly on the birds, they also use their ponytails.
Speaker Howardsays : What’s your point?

Speaker Sheldonsays : It amazes me how you constantly obsess over fictional details when there are more important things in the real world to worry about. For example, why wasn’t William Shatner in the new Star Trek movie?
Speaker Leonardsays : Hey, Sheldon, I was up in the administration office, and I happened to overhear the name of the winner of this year’s Chancellor’s Award for Science.
Speaker Sheldonsays : And you want to rub my nose in the fact that my contributions are being overlooked again? I am the William Shatner of theoretical physics. All right, I’ll play. What self-important, preening fraud are they honouring this year?
Speaker Leonardsays : Oh, I’m so glad you asked it like that. You.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I won?
Speaker Leonardsays : You won.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I won! This is astonishing. Not that I won the award, no one deserves it more. Actually, I guess I misspoke. It’s not astonishing, more like inevitable. I’m not sure what to do first. Maybe I should call my mother. Wait! I know, I’m going to conduct an interview with myself and post it online.
Speaker Rajsays : Well, good for him.
Speaker Howardsays : Yeah, the one thing the William Shatner of theoretical physics needed was an ego boost.

Coversation [March 25, 2010, Session #436]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Good evening, ladies and gentlemen. My name is Dr. Leonard Hofstadter, and it is my very great honour to introduce the winner of this year’s Chancellor’s award for Science and my good friend, Dr. Sheldon Cooper. But before I do, I’d like to share with you a letter from Sheldon’s mother, who couldn’t be here tonight. Isn’t that nice? His mother sent him a letter. She’s proud of him. I wonder what that feels like. Dear Shelly. That’s what she calls him. Shelly, it’s a pet name. You know what my mother’s pet name for me is? Leonard. But I digress. Dear Shelly. I am so proud of…
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, dear.
Speaker Pennysays : What’s the matter?
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m getting dizzy.
Speaker Rajsays : Don’t worry. You’re surrounded by your C-Men.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I can’t do this. I’m going to faint.
Speaker Pennysays : Here, drink this. It’ll relax you.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Alcohol? I don’t drink alcohol.
Speaker Pennysays : Fine, faint.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I don’t feel different, this alcohol’s defective.
Speaker Pennysays : Here, see if this one works.
Speaker talking)says : First of all, the projects were totally different. I was showing that classical music nurtures lima beans and makes them grow, but my mother didn’t hear me. If you’d like to look at the relationship between nurturing and growth, I’d like to point out that my brother is eight inches taller than me.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m ready.
Speaker Leonardsays : Oh, right. Ladies and gentlemen, our guest of honour, Dr. Sheldon Cooper.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Thanks, shorty,I’ll take it from here. All right, you people ready to have some fun? I can hear you metabolizing oxygen and expelling carbon dioxide. Looks like we have some academic dignitaries in the audience. Dr. Randall from the geology department, only man who’s happy when they take his work for granite. Ba-da cha! I kid the geologists, of course, but it’s only ’cause I have no respect for the field. Let’s get serious for a moment. Why are we all here? ‘Cause we’re scientists. And what do scientists study? The universe. And what’s the universe made of? I am so glad you asked.  There’s antimony, arsenic, aluminium, selenium, and hydrogen and oxygen and nitrogen and rhenium, and nickel, neodymium,

Coversation [November 17, 2011, Session #709]:
Speaker Leonardsays : Hey, can I talk to you about something? It’s a little awkward.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I know what this is about. Given the professional standstill you’re at, you’re wondering if this is the appropriate time to abandon your research and focus on teaching. Yes. And if I may suggest, consider changing disciplines. Yeah, to the humanities, perhaps history. One of the advantages of teaching history is that you don’t have to create things, you know, you just have to remember stuff that happened and then parrot it back. You could have fun with that.
Speaker Leonardsays : Yeah, that’s not it. Stuart’s kind of interested in Amy.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, of course he is. She’s very interesting. Did you know, when she was 14, she severed the webbing between her own toes?
Speaker Leonardsays : No. He wanted me to find out if you’d have a problem with him asking her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : I’m not sure how to respond, Leonard. I don’t own Amy. Can’t own a person. At least not since? 1863. President Lincoln freed the? Slaves! Come on Leonard, if you’re going to teach history, these are the kind of facts you’ll have to know.
Speaker Leonardsays : You know what? Never mind. I’m going to tell him it’s okay to ask her out.
Speaker Sheldonsays : The question is moot. Speaker Leonardsays : All right, but just for the record, I checked in with you to see how you’d feel about it.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Fine. The record shall so reflect. Now, getting back to the problem at hand, what to do with a washed-up experimental physicist.
Speaker Leonardsays : I am not washed-up.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, Leonard. Lots of people love you and want to help you, but they can’t until you admit the problem.

Coversation [March 19, 2008, Session #64]:
Speaker Leonardsays : So, in conclusion, the data show that at temperatures approaching absolute zero, the moment of inertia changes, and the solid becomes a super-solid, which clearly appears to be a previously unknown state of matter.  Thank you.  Are there any questions?
Speaker Voicesays : Yeah. What the hell was that?
Speaker Leonardsays : Any other questions?
Speaker glasses)says : Doctor Sheldon Cooper here, I am the lead author of this particular paper.  Thank you. And you, sir, you have completely skipped over the part where I was walking through the park, and I saw these children on a merry-go-round, which started me thinking about the moment of inertia in gasses like helium at temperatures approaching absolute zero.
Speaker Leonardsays : I didn’t skip it, it’s just an anecdote. It’s not science.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, I see, was the apple falling on Newton’s head, was that just an anecdote?
Speaker Leonardsays : You are not Isaac Newton.
Speaker Sheldonsays : No, no that’s true, gravity would have been apparent to me without the apple.
Speaker Leonardsays : You cannot possibly be that arrogant.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You continue to underestimate me, my good man.
Speaker Leonardsays : Look, if you weren’t happy with my presentation then maybe you should have given it with me.
Speaker Sheldonsays : As I have explained repeatedly, unlike you, I don’t need validation from lesser minds. No offence.
Speaker Leonardsays : Really, so why did you come?
Speaker Sheldonsays : Because I knew you’d screw this up.
Speaker Leonardsays : I didn’t screw it up.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh, please. I admit, that spherical chicken joke, that was hilarious. But it was straight downhill from there.
Speaker Leonardsays : I’ve had enough of your condescension. Maybe I didn’t go to college when I was eleven like you, maybe I got my doctorate at 24 instead of 16, but you are not the only person who is smarter than everyone else in this room. No offense. And I am clearly not the only person who is tormented by insecurity and has an ego in need of constant validation.
Speaker Sheldonsays : So you admit that you’re an egotist?
Speaker Leonardsays : Yes.  My name is Dr Leonard Hofstadter, and I could never please my parents so I need to get all my self-esteem from strangers like you. But he’s worse.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Okay, that is it.
Speaker Leonardsays : You cannot blow up my head with your mind.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Then I’ll settle for an aneurysm.
Speaker down)says : Stop it.
Speaker Sheldonsays : You hit me. You saw him, he hit me.
Speaker Leonardsays : You were trying to blow up my head.
Speaker Sheldonsays : So it was working.
Speaker Leonardsays : It wasn’t, it was not, you are a nutcase.
Speaker Sheldonsays : Oh we’ll see about that , heads up you people in the front row, this is a splash zone.
Speaker Leonardsays : Stop, stop it, quit it.
Speaker Pennysays : Is this usually how these physics things go?
Speaker Howardsays : More often than you’d think.
Speaker floor)says : Vulcan nerve pinch!
